one_line_definition: Science of interacting surfaces in relative motion, encompassing friction, wear, and lubrication
Manufacturing processes (grinding, honing, lapping, polishing) where material removal is by abrasive action
Most common abrasive machining process using bonded abrasive wheels to remove material from workpieces
Ratio of workpiece volume removed to abrasive wheel volume lost, a measure of wheel efficiency
Depth to which an individual abrasive grain penetrates the workpiece surface during a cutting pass
Structured set of interacting tribological elements (abrasive, workpiece, fluid) analyzed as a system
Study of stresses and deformations at the interface between contacting bodies, applied here to abrasive contacts
Wear mechanism where hard asperities or particles slide directly between two surfaces
Wear mechanism where loose abrasive particles roll and slide between two surfaces
Metallurgical damage (oxidation, re-hardening, softening) caused by excessive heat in the grinding zone
Computational technique simulating atomic-scale interactions to study nanoscale abrasion and deformation
Partial differential equation governing pressure distribution in a thin lubricant film between moving surfaces
Empirical measure of how much a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature; higher VI = less change
Lubrication regime where surfaces are fully separated by a pressurized fluid film generated by relative motion
Lubrication regime where elastic deformation of surfaces and pressure-dependent viscosity both significantly affect film thickness
Lubrication regime where surfaces are in partial contact, protected by adsorbed or chemically reacted molecular films
Lubrication under very high loads using additives that form sacrificial reaction films on metal surfaces
Cylindrical plain bearing where a rotating shaft (journal) is supported by a hydrodynamic oil film
Dimensionless parameter combining load, speed, viscosity, and geometry used to characterize journal bearing performance
Elastic contact stress theory for bodies with curved surfaces under normal load, predicting contact area and pressure distribution
Transient temperature rise at asperity contact points during sliding, influencing lubricant film stability
Mathematical transformation of the Reynolds equation that improves numerical stability for finite-difference solutions
Extension of hydrodynamic lubrication analysis that accounts for heat generation and temperature-dependent viscosity within the film
Lubrication using externally pressurized fluid supplied to bearing pockets, providing load support even at zero speed
Industry standard classification of lubricant viscosity at defined temperatures, specified by the Society of Automotive Engineers
Semi-solid lubricant consisting of a base oil dispersed in a thickener, used where fluid lubricants cannot be retained
Engine that converts chemical energy of fuel combustion into mechanical work within the working fluid itself
Ratio of cylinder bore diameter to piston stroke length, influencing combustion efficiency, engine height, and piston speed
Graphical representation of engine torque, power, and BSFC across the speed-load operating range
Oscillatory twisting vibrations in the crankshaft driven by cyclic combustion pressure pulses, managed by tuned dampeners
Dynamic behavior of piston rings in the groove, including flutter and collapse that affect oil consumption and blow-by
Methodology for predicting component life under cyclic loading using stress-life (S-N) curves and mean stress corrections
Split cylindrical bearing relying on hydrodynamic oil film to support rotating shaft loads in engine applications
Rotating shaft carrying lobes that open and close intake and exhaust valves according to the engine's timing requirements
Increasing engine air charge density using exhaust-driven (turbo) or mechanically driven (super) compressors to boost power
Electronic device using semiconductor junctions to convert alternating current to direct current
